question_text: A distance-time graph for a runner shows a straight line from (0, 0) to (10, 100), then a horizontal line from (10, 100) to (15, 100). Another runner's graph shows a straight line from (0, 0) to (15, 150). Compare the average speeds of both runners and explain which runner was faster during the first 10 seconds.
correct_answer: Runner 1: average speed = 100 ÷ 15 = 6.67 m/s. Runner 2: average speed = 150 ÷ 15 = 10 m/s. During the first 10 seconds, Runner 1 had a speed of 10 m/s (100 m ÷ 10 s) and Runner 2 had a speed of 10 m/s (150 m ÷ 15 s, so 10 m/s for entire journey). Runner 1 was as fast or faster in the first 10 seconds.
explanation: The first runner travels 100 m in 10 seconds (speed = 10 m/s), then stops. The second runner travels 150 m in 15 seconds uniformly (speed = 10 m/s throughout). Both travelled at the same instantaneous speed for the first 10 seconds, but Runner 1's average speed over the full 15 seconds is lower because they stopped.
